title: 学中文最头疼的三个词：“了、把、就”

youtube.publish_title: Mastering Chinese Grammar: 了, 把, 就 | 学中文最头疼的三个词

chapters.title: 最让人头疼的三个小词
打破误区：“了”不仅仅是过去式
核心逻辑：什么时候必须用“把”？
无处不在的“就”：从时间到强调
综合运用：有和没有它们，听起来差多少？

in_this_episode_you_will_learn: Why Chinese relies heavily on particles rather than verb conjugations.
How to use 'le' (了) to indicate a change of state, not just past events.
The core logic of the 'ba' (把) structure for handling and disposing of objects.
The diverse meanings of 'jiu' (就), from indicating earliness to strong emphasis.
Practical tips for building muscle memory with conversational Chinese phrases.
description_intro: Welcome to another episode of our Chinese learning podcast! Today, Panpan and Lao Lu are tackling three of the most notorious words in Mandarin Chinese: 了 (le), 把 (ba), and 就 (jiu). While they might seem tiny and insignificant, these three particles are essential for making your Chinese sound natural and expressive.

Many learners freeze up when trying to use them, often because there are no direct English translations. Instead of burying you in confusing textbook grammar trees, we break down these concepts through relatable everyday scenarios, funny examples, and clear logic. Let's conquer these 'mountains' together!

blocks.purpose: 开场白，建立亲切感并抛出本集核心话题

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 大家好，欢迎收听我们的中文播客。我是盼盼。
segments.speech_text: [cheerfully] 大家好，欢迎收听我们的中文播客。我是盼盼。
segments.en: Hello everyone, welcome to our Chinese podcast. I'm Panpan.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 大家好，我是老路。盼盼，今天我们要聊点什么？
segments.speech_text: 大家好，我是老路。盼盼，今天我们要聊点什么？
segments.en: Hello everyone, I'm Lao Lu. Panpan, what are we going to talk about today?

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 路哥，今天我们要聊一个让所有学中文的朋友，都非常头疼的话题。
segments.speech_text: [playfully] 路哥，今天我们要聊一个让所有学中文的朋友，都非常头疼的话题。
segments.en: Lao Lu, today we're going to talk about a topic that gives all Chinese learners a major headache.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 哦？是非常难的发音吗？还是复杂的成语？
segments.speech_text: [curious] 哦？是非常难的发音吗？还是复杂的成语？
segments.en: Oh? Is it very difficult pronunciation? Or complex idioms?

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 都不是。是三个看起来非常小，非常简单，但一用就容易错的词。
segments.speech_text: 都不是。是三个看起来非常小，非常简单，但一用就容易错的词。
segments.en: Neither. It's three words that look very small and simple, but are incredibly easy to use incorrectly.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 让我猜猜看，是不是“了、把、就”这三个老熟人？
segments.speech_text: 让我猜猜看，是不是“了、把、就”这三个老熟人？
segments.en: Let me guess, is it our old friends 'le', 'ba', and 'jiu'?

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 没错！就是这三个。我最近好几个学中文的朋友都在跟我抱怨。
segments.speech_text: [excited] 没错！就是这三个。我最近好几个学中文的朋友都在跟我抱怨。
segments.en: Exactly! It's those three. Several of my friends who are learning Chinese have been complaining to me about them recently.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 确实，这三个词在中文里出现频率极高，可以说是绕不开的“三座大山”。
segments.speech_text: [calmly] 确实，这三个词在中文里出现频率极高，可以说是绕不开的“三座大山”。
segments.en: Indeed, these three words appear with extremely high frequency in Chinese. You could say they are unavoidable 'mountains' to cross.

blocks.purpose: 探讨学习者对这些词的心理障碍，解释中文依赖虚词表达状态的逻辑

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 我有一个朋友，他现在的词汇量其实挺大的了，但是一开口说话，遇到这三个词就卡壳。
segments.speech_text: [thoughtful] 我有一个朋友，他现在的词汇量其实挺大的了，但是一开口说话，遇到这三个词就卡壳。
segments.en: I have a friend whose vocabulary is actually quite large now, but whenever he starts speaking and encounters these three words, he completely freezes up.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 卡壳是因为他在脑子里找对应的英文翻译，对吧？
segments.speech_text: 卡壳是因为他在脑子里找对应的英文翻译，对吧？
segments.en: He freezes up because he's trying to find the direct English translation in his head, right?

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 对，他说英文里根本没有这种东西。他问我，能不能干脆不用它们？
segments.speech_text: 对，他说英文里根本没有这种东西。他问我，能不能干脆不用它们？
segments.en: Yes, he said there's simply no equivalent in English. He asked me if he could just skip using them altogether.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 其实，如果不加这些词，很多时候意思也是能猜出来的，但听起来就会非常生硬。
segments.speech_text: 其实，如果不加这些词，很多时候意思也是能猜出来的，但听起来就会非常生硬。
segments.en: Actually, if you don't add these words, people can still guess the meaning most of the time, but it will sound very unnatural.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 就像是没有加盐的菜，对吧？虽然能吃，但完全没有味道。
segments.speech_text: [soft laugh] 就像是没有加盐的菜，对吧？虽然能吃，但完全没有味道。
segments.en: It's like cooking a dish without adding any salt, right? You can eat it, but it's completely flavorless.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 这个比喻非常准确。中文不靠动词变位来表达状态，而是靠这些小词来传递语气和细节。
segments.speech_text: [reassuringly] 这个比喻非常准确。中文不靠动词变位来表达状态，而是靠这些小词来传递语气和细节。
segments.en: That analogy is perfectly accurate. Chinese doesn't rely on verb conjugations to express states; instead, it uses these tiny words to convey tone and subtle details.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 所以，想把中文说得地道、有感情，这三个词是必须要掌握的。
segments.speech_text: 所以，想把中文说得地道、有感情，这三个词是必须要掌握的。
segments.en: So, if you want to speak Chinese authentically and with emotion, mastering these three words is an absolute must.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 那我们今天就一点一点地把它们拆开来看，不用死记硬背语法，咱们凭感觉来。
segments.speech_text: 那我们今天就一点一点地把它们拆开来看，不用死记硬背语法，咱们凭感觉来。
segments.en: Then let's break them down bit by bit today. No need to memorize strict grammar rules; let's go by feeling.

blocks.purpose: 探讨“了”表示“状态变化”的用法，纠正“了等于过去式”的误区

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 好，那我们先来对付第一个大魔王：“了”。
segments.speech_text: [cheerfully] 好，那我们先来对付第一个大魔王：“了”。
segments.en: Alright, let's tackle the first final boss: 'le'.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 很多初学者对“了”的第一个印象，就是它等于英文的过去式。
segments.speech_text: 很多初学者对“了”的第一个印象，就是它等于英文的过去式。
segments.en: The first impression many beginners have of 'le' is that it equals the English past tense.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 对，我以前辅导留学生的时候，他们造句都是“昨天我去了超市”，“昨天我吃了苹果”。
segments.speech_text: 对，我以前辅导留学生的时候，他们造句都是“昨天我去了超市”，“昨天我吃了苹果”。
segments.en: Yes, when I used to tutor international students, their sentences were always 'Yesterday I went [le] to the supermarket', 'Yesterday I ate [le] an apple'.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 这样用其实不能算错，但“了”远远不止是过去式那么简单。
segments.speech_text: [calmly] 这样用其实不能算错，但“了”远远不止是过去式那么简单。
segments.en: Using it that way isn't actually wrong, but 'le' is far more than just a simple past tense marker.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 是的，我后来发现，我们平时用“了”最多的时候，其实是在表达一种“变化”。
segments.speech_text: 是的，我后来发现，我们平时用“了”最多的时候，其实是在表达一种“变化”。
segments.en: Exactly, I realized later that the most frequent way we use 'le' in daily life is actually to express a 'change'.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 没错，状态的变化。盼盼，你能不能给大家举个最常见的例子？
segments.speech_text: 没错，状态的变化。盼盼，你能不能给大家举个最常见的例子？
segments.en: Right, a change of state. Panpan, could you give everyone a common example?

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 比如，我们经常说“下雨了”。这不代表雨下完了，而是说，刚才没下雨，现在开始下了。
segments.speech_text: [confident] 比如，我们经常说“下雨了”。这不代表雨下完了，而是说，刚才没下雨，现在开始下了。
segments.en: For instance, we often say 'xia yu le' (It's raining). This doesn't mean the rain is over; it means it wasn't raining a moment ago, and now it has started.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 对，这是一个新情况。再比如，你到了下午，肚子叫了，你会怎么说？
segments.speech_text: 对，这是一个新情况。再比如，你到了下午，肚子叫了，你会怎么说？
segments.en: Yes, that's a new situation. Or for example, it's the afternoon, your stomach growls, what would you say?

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 我会说，“我饿了”。意思是之前不饿，现在状态改变了，我需要吃东西了。
segments.speech_text: 我会说，“我饿了”。意思是之前不饿，现在状态改变了，我需要吃东西了。
segments.en: I would say, 'I'm hungry now'. It means I wasn't hungry before, but my state has changed, and I need to eat.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 非常好。所以大家要记住，“了”经常用来告诉你，情况和以前不一样了。
segments.speech_text: [reassuringly] 非常好。所以大家要记住，“了”经常用来告诉你，情况和以前不一样了。
segments.en: Excellent. So everyone should remember, 'le' is often used to tell you that the situation is different from before.

blocks.purpose: 讲解“了”表示动作完成的用法，以及强调习惯性动作不加“了”的规则

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 那放在动词后面的“了”呢？是不是就真的是过去式了？
segments.speech_text: [curious] 那放在动词后面的“了”呢？是不是就真的是过去式了？
segments.en: What about the 'le' placed right after a verb? Does that really just mean past tense then?

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 放在动词后面，主要是为了强调一个动作已经“完成”了。
segments.speech_text: 放在动词后面，主要是为了强调一个动作已经“完成”了。
segments.en: Placing it after a verb is primarily to emphasize that an action has been 'completed'.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 强调完成。这个点其实挺容易搞混的。
segments.speech_text: 强调完成。这个点其实挺容易搞混的。
segments.en: Emphasizing completion. This point is actually quite easy to get mixed up.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 是的，特别是遇到习惯性动作的时候。比如，有人会说“我每天都喝了咖啡”。
segments.speech_text: 是的，特别是遇到习惯性动作的时候。比如，有人会说“我每天都喝了咖啡”。
segments.en: Yes, especially when dealing with habitual actions. For instance, some people might say 'I drink [le] coffee every day'.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 哎呀，这个听起来太别扭了。如果是每天都做的事情，我们一般不会加“了”。
segments.speech_text: [laughs] 哎呀，这个听起来太别扭了。如果是每天都做的事情，我们一般不会加“了”。
segments.en: Oh my, that sounds so awkward. If it's something done every day, we generally wouldn't add 'le'.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 对，每天都喝，说明它是一个日常习惯，并不是强调某一次喝完的动作，所以直接说“我每天都喝咖啡”就可以了。
segments.speech_text: 对，每天都喝，说明它是一个日常习惯，并不是强调某一次喝完的动作，所以直接说“我每天都喝咖啡”就可以了。
segments.en: Right. Drinking it every day indicates it's a daily habit, not emphasizing the completion of a single drinking event, so you can just say 'I drink coffee every day'.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 懂了。所以“了”关注的是那一个动作结束的瞬间，而不是一个长期的习惯。
segments.speech_text: [thoughtful] 懂了。所以“了”关注的是那一个动作结束的瞬间，而不是一个长期的习惯。
segments.en: Got it. So 'le' focuses on that specific moment an action concludes, not on a long-term habit.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 总结得很到位。只要不是强调动作完成，或者状态改变，很多时候是可以不用“了”的。
segments.speech_text: [warmly] 总结得很到位。只要不是强调动作完成，或者状态改变，很多时候是可以不用“了”的。
segments.en: That's a very solid summary. As long as you aren't emphasizing the completion of an action or a change in state, many times you can just skip using 'le'.

blocks.purpose: 引入“把”字句的核心逻辑：对特定对象的处置和结果

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 过了“了”这一关，咱们再来看看“把”。这个词我朋友更崩溃。
segments.speech_text: 过了“了”这一关，咱们再来看看“把”。这个词我朋友更崩溃。
segments.en: Now that we've passed the 'le' hurdle, let's take a look at 'ba'. My friend gets even more frustrated with this word.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: “把”字句绝对是中文语法里的重头戏。它为什么让人崩溃呢？
segments.speech_text: “把”字句绝对是中文语法里的重头戏。它为什么让人崩溃呢？
segments.en: The 'ba' structure is definitely a heavy hitter in Chinese grammar. Why does it frustrate people so much?

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 因为大家觉得多此一举啊。明明可以说“我喝咖啡”，为什么非要说“我把咖啡喝了”？
segments.speech_text: [curious] 因为大家觉得多此一举啊。明明可以说“我喝咖啡”，为什么非要说“我把咖啡喝了”？
segments.en: Because everyone feels it's redundant. When you can clearly just say 'I drink coffee', why do you have to say 'I take the coffee and drink it'?

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 这是一个非常好的问题。其实，用不用“把”，取决于你想强调什么。
segments.speech_text: 这是一个非常好的问题。其实，用不用“把”，取决于你想强调什么。
segments.en: That's a really good question. Actually, whether you use 'ba' or not depends on what you want to emphasize.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 强调什么？是强调那个东西，还是强调动作？
segments.speech_text: 强调什么？是强调那个东西，还是强调动作？
segments.en: Emphasize what? Emphasize the object, or the action?

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 主要是强调对一个“特定对象”的“处置”，以及产生的结果。
segments.speech_text: 主要是强调对一个“特定对象”的“处置”，以及产生的结果。
segments.en: It mainly emphasizes the 'disposal' or handling of a 'specific object', and the resulting outcome.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 处置？听起来有点严肃。能不能用生活里的大白话解释一下？
segments.speech_text: [awkwardly] 处置？听起来有点严肃。能不能用生活里的大白话解释一下？
segments.en: Disposal? That sounds a bit serious. Could you explain it using everyday plain language?

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 简单来说，就是你把一个已知的东西怎么样了。比如那杯咖啡就在桌上，你拿起来，喝掉了，让它变空了。
segments.speech_text: 简单来说，就是你把一个已知的东西怎么样了。比如那杯咖啡就在桌上，你拿起来，喝掉了，让它变空了。
segments.en: Simply put, it's about what you do to a known object. For instance, there's a cup of coffee right on the table. You pick it up and drink it, leaving it empty.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 哦，就是说那个咖啡是我特定指的那杯，而且重点是我把它消灭掉了，对吧？
segments.speech_text: 哦，就是说那个咖啡是我特定指的那杯，而且重点是我把它消灭掉了，对吧？
segments.en: Oh, so it means that coffee is that specific cup I'm referring to, and the focal point is that I eliminated it, right?

blocks.purpose: 展示“把”字句在日常做家务、下指令中的应用，以及“动词后不能空着”的结构规则

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 完全正确。“我喝咖啡”只是陈述一件事；“我把咖啡喝了”强调的是那杯咖啡的结局。
segments.speech_text: [confidently] 完全正确。“我喝咖啡”只是陈述一件事；“我把咖啡喝了”强调的是那杯咖啡的结局。
segments.en: Exactly right. 'I drink coffee' merely states a fact; 'I ba the coffee drank' emphasizes the final fate of that cup of coffee.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 我突然发现，我们在生活中下指令、或者分配任务的时候，特别喜欢用“把”。
segments.speech_text: [surprised] 我突然发现，我们在生活中下指令、或者分配任务的时候，特别喜欢用“把”。
segments.en: I just suddenly realized that in our daily lives, when we give commands or assign tasks, we especially love using 'ba'.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 是吗？你联想到了哪些场景？
segments.speech_text: 是吗？你联想到了哪些场景？
segments.en: Is that so? What scenarios came to mind?

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 比如妈妈经常对孩子说，“把房间打扫干净”，“把作业写完”，“把电视关掉”。
segments.speech_text: 比如妈妈经常对孩子说，“把房间打扫干净”，“把作业写完”，“把电视关掉”。
segments.en: For example, moms often say to their kids, 'Clean the room', 'Finish your homework', 'Turn off the TV' (all using the 'ba' structure).

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 没错，这些都是非常典型的“把”字句。都是要求你对某个东西采取动作，并达到一个结果。
segments.speech_text: 没错，这些都是非常典型的“把”字句。都是要求你对某个东西采取动作，并达到一个结果。
segments.en: That's right, those are very typical 'ba' sentences. They are all demanding that you take action on a certain object and achieve a result.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 而且我还注意到一个规律，用了“把”之后，动词后面一般都不能空着，得加点什么。
segments.speech_text: 而且我还注意到一个规律，用了“把”之后，动词后面一般都不能空着，得加点什么。
segments.en: And I also noticed a pattern: after using 'ba', the space after the verb generally can't be left empty; you have to add something.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 盼盼的语感非常敏锐。确实，我们不能只说“把门关”，必须得说“把门关上”。
segments.speech_text: [warmly] 盼盼的语感非常敏锐。确实，我们不能只说“把门关”，必须得说“把门关上”。
segments.en: Panpan's language sense is very sharp. Indeed, we can't just say 'ba the door close'; we have to say 'ba the door close shut'.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 不能说“把书看”，得说“把书看完”。后面必须要有一个结果或者方向。
segments.speech_text: 不能说“把书看”，得说“把书看完”。后面必须要有一个结果或者方向。
segments.en: You can't say 'ba the book read', you must say 'ba the book read finish'. There must be a result or direction following it.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 对，记住这个小窍门，动词后面一定要跟个“小尾巴”，你的“把”字句就基本不会错了。
segments.speech_text: 对，记住这个小窍门，动词后面一定要跟个“小尾巴”，你的“把”字句就基本不会错了。
segments.en: Correct. Just remember this little trick: the verb must always be followed by a 'little tail', and your 'ba' sentences will rarely be wrong.

blocks.purpose: 讲解“就”在表示时间早、动作快以及动作紧密连接时的用法

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 最后一个大魔王，也就是“就”。我觉得“就”像个变色龙，在不同语境里意思完全不一样。
segments.speech_text: [sigh] 最后一个大魔王，也就是“就”。我觉得“就”像个变色龙，在不同语境里意思完全不一样。
segments.en: The final major boss is 'jiu'. I feel like 'jiu' is a chameleon; its meaning is completely different depending on the context.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: “就”确实非常灵活。在 HSK3 这个阶段，大家最先接触到的，应该是表示时间或者动作发生得“早、快、顺利”。
segments.speech_text: “就”确实非常灵活。在 HSK3 这个阶段，大家最先接触到的，应该是表示时间或者动作发生得“早、快、顺利”。
segments.en: 'Jiu' is indeed very flexible. At the HSK3 stage, the first usage everyone encounters is likely expressing that a time or action happens 'early, quickly, or smoothly'.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 对，比如我们约了八点见面，你七点半到了。我就会惊讶地说，“你这么早就到了？”
segments.speech_text: 对，比如我们约了八点见面，你七点半到了。我就会惊讶地说，“你这么早就到了？”
segments.en: Right, for example, if we arranged to meet at 8 o'clock, but you arrived at 7:30. I would say in surprise, 'You arrived so early?'

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 然后我可能会自豪地回答，“是啊，我七点就出门了。”
segments.speech_text: [playfully] 然后我可能会自豪地回答，“是啊，我七点就出门了。”
segments.en: And I might proudly reply, 'Yeah, I headed out at 7 o'clock.' (using jiu to emphasize earliness)

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 这里的“就”带有很强的主观情绪。如果我们去掉它，变成“我七点出门，七点半到了”。
segments.speech_text: 这里的“就”带有很强的主观情绪。如果我们去掉它，变成“我七点出门，七点半到了”。
segments.en: The 'jiu' here carries a very strong subjective emotion. If we remove it and change it to 'I headed out at 7, arrived at 7:30'...

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 那听起来就像是在给警察录口供，非常客观，非常冷漠。
segments.speech_text: [laughs] 那听起来就像是在给警察录口供，非常客观，非常冷漠。
segments.en: Then it sounds like you're giving a statement to the police—extremely objective and very cold.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 加上“就”，立刻就能感觉到那种“快”和“顺利”的感觉。
segments.speech_text: 加上“就”，立刻就能感觉到那种“快”和“顺利”的感觉。
segments.en: Adding 'jiu' instantly brings out that feeling of 'speed' and 'smoothness'.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 另外，“就”还可以表示两个动作连接得很紧密。比如“我下班就去健身房”。
segments.speech_text: 另外，“就”还可以表示两个动作连接得很紧密。比如“我下班就去健身房”。
segments.en: Additionally, 'jiu' can indicate that two actions are connected very tightly. For example, 'I will go to the gym right after work'.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 中间不耽误时间，马上就去的意思，对吧？这个在日常计划里太实用了。
segments.speech_text: 中间不耽误时间，马上就去的意思，对吧？这个在日常计划里太实用了。
segments.en: Meaning no time is wasted in between, going immediately, right? This is incredibly practical for daily plans.

blocks.purpose: 讲解“就”在表示“仅仅”以及强烈主观强调时的用法

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 是的。不过，“就”还有另外一个很常用的功能，表示“仅仅”或者强调。
segments.speech_text: 是的。不过，“就”还有另外一个很常用的功能，表示“仅仅”或者强调。
segments.en: Yes. However, 'jiu' has another very common function, which is to express 'only' or to add emphasis.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 这个我也常用。比如我上街买东西，一摸口袋，哎呀，我就带了一百块钱。
segments.speech_text: [laughs] 这个我也常用。比如我上街买东西，一摸口袋，哎呀，我就带了一百块钱。
segments.en: I use this a lot too. For instance, if I go out shopping, touch my pocket and go, 'Oh no, I ONLY brought 100 RMB.'

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 这里的“就”等于“只”，强调数量很少。如果我说，“屋里就他一个人”，也是一样的意思。
segments.speech_text: 这里的“就”等于“只”，强调数量很少。如果我说，“屋里就他一个人”，也是一样的意思。
segments.en: The 'jiu' here equals 'zhi' (only), emphasizing a small amount. If I say, 'There is only him alone in the room,' it's the exact same meaning.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 还有一种更强烈的强调。比如大家在找谁是内鬼，最后指着一个人说：“就是他！”
segments.speech_text: [excited] 还有一种更强烈的强调。比如大家在找谁是内鬼，最后指着一个人说：“就是他！”
segments.en: There's an even stronger emphasis. Like when everyone is trying to find out who the mole is, and finally someone points at a person and says, 'It's HIM!'

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 这种语气非常坚定，没有任何犹豫。“就在这儿”、“就是这样”、“就买这个”。
segments.speech_text: [confidently] 这种语气非常坚定，没有任何犹豫。“就在这儿”、“就是这样”、“就买这个”。
segments.en: This tone is incredibly firm, without any hesitation whatsoever. 'It's right here', 'It's exactly like this', 'Just buy this one'.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 我觉得这种表达特别爽快。学会用这种“就”，感觉中文水平瞬间提升了一个档次。
segments.speech_text: 我觉得这种表达特别爽快。学会用这种“就”，感觉中文水平瞬间提升了一个档次。
segments.en: I feel like this kind of expression is so satisfying. Once you learn to use this 'jiu', it feels like your Chinese level instantly jumps up a tier.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 没错，当你开始自然而然地用“就”来表达态度的时候，你的中文就非常有生命力了。
segments.speech_text: 没错，当你开始自然而然地用“就”来表达态度的时候，你的中文就非常有生命力了。
segments.en: That's right, when you start naturally using 'jiu' to express your attitude, your Chinese becomes full of vitality.

blocks.purpose: 通过对比有和没有这些词的句子，展示它们如何让语言变得“有人情味”

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 聊了这么多，路哥，我们要不要把这三个词放在一起，给听众朋友们一个更直观的感受？
segments.speech_text: [cheerfully] 聊了这么多，路哥，我们要不要把这三个词放在一起，给听众朋友们一个更直观的感受？
segments.en: Having talked about all this, Lao Lu, should we put these three words together to give our listeners a more intuitive feel?

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 好主意。我们来对比一下两种表达方式。盼盼，你先说一个没有这三个词的句子。
segments.speech_text: 好主意。我们来对比一下两种表达方式。盼盼，你先说一个没有这三个词的句子。
segments.en: Good idea. Let's compare two ways of expressing something. Panpan, you first say a sentence without these three words.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 好的。比如：“我做作业。我看电视。”
segments.speech_text: 好的。比如：“我做作业。我看电视。”
segments.en: Okay. For instance: 'I do homework. I watch TV.'

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 听懂是完全没问题的。但听起来就像两个完全没有关联的机器人指令。
segments.speech_text: [laughs] 听懂是完全没问题的。但听起来就像两个完全没有关联的机器人指令。
segments.en: There's absolutely no problem understanding it. But it sounds like two completely unrelated robot commands.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 那如果加上我们今天学的“了、把、就”呢？
segments.speech_text: 那如果加上我们今天学的“了、把、就”呢？
segments.en: And what if we add the 'le', 'ba', and 'jiu' we learned today?

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 那就可以说：“我把作业做完了，就去看电视了。”
segments.speech_text: 那就可以说：“我把作业做完了，就去看电视了。”
segments.en: Then you could say: 'Once I finished [ba] my homework [le], I went [jiu] to watch TV [le].'

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 哇，一听就是个活生生的人在说话！而且画面感特别强。
segments.speech_text: [surprised] 哇，一听就是个活生生的人在说话！而且画面感特别强。
segments.en: Wow, immediately it sounds like a living, breathing person speaking! And the imagery is very strong.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 是的。“把”引出了处置对象作业，“完”是结果，“就”连接了下一个动作，最后的“了”表示新情况的发生。
segments.speech_text: [calmly] 是的。“把”引出了处置对象作业，“完”是结果，“就”连接了下一个动作，最后的“了”表示新情况的发生。
segments.en: Yes. 'Ba' introduces the object being handled—the homework; 'wan' (finish) is the result; 'jiu' connects to the next action; and the final 'le' indicates the occurrence of a new situation.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 完美串联。通过这个对比，大家应该能体会到这三个词的魔力了。
segments.speech_text: 完美串联。通过这个对比，大家应该能体会到这三个词的魔力了。
segments.en: Perfectly linked together. Through this comparison, everyone should be able to appreciate the magic of these three words.

blocks.purpose: 提出学习策略：语块记忆法，鼓励学习者不要在交流时过度纠结语法

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 可是路哥，听你讲的时候我觉得都懂了，但我自己要用的时候，脑子里去过这么多语法规则，肯定来不及啊。
segments.speech_text: [hesitantly] 可是路哥，听你讲的时候我觉得都懂了，但我自己要用的时候，脑子里去过这么多语法规则，肯定来不及啊。
segments.en: But Lao Lu, when listening to you explain I feel like I understand it all, but when I need to use it myself, filtering through all these grammar rules in my head definitely takes too long.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 这是一个非常普遍的焦虑。我的建议是，永远不要在说话的瞬间去推导语法公式。
segments.speech_text: 这是一个非常普遍的焦虑。我的建议是，永远不要在说话的瞬间去推导语法公式。
segments.en: That is a very common anxiety. My advice is: never try to deduce grammar formulas in the exact moment you're speaking.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 那我们该怎么办呢？靠本能吗？
segments.speech_text: 那我们该怎么办呢？靠本能吗？
segments.en: So what should we do? Rely on instinct?

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 靠语块，或者说固定搭配。比如你不需要去分析“知道了”为什么有“了”，你只需要把“知道了”当作一个整体去用。
segments.speech_text: 靠语块，或者说固定搭配。比如你不需要去分析“知道了”为什么有“了”，你只需要把“知道了”当作一个整体去用。
segments.en: Rely on chunks, or fixed collocations. For example, you don't need to analyze why 'zhi dao le' (got it/understood) has a 'le', you just need to use 'zhi dao le' as a single unit.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 哦，就是把它当成一个单词来记。
segments.speech_text: 哦，就是把它当成一个单词来记。
segments.en: Oh, so just memorize it like it's one single vocabulary word.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 对。比如别人交给你任务，你说“把它交给我”；你答应马上过去，你说“这就来”。
segments.speech_text: 对。比如别人交给你任务，你说“把它交给我”；你答应马上过去，你说“这就来”。
segments.en: Right. For instance, when someone hands you a task, you say 'leave it to me' (ba ta jiao gei wo); when you promise to go over right away, you say 'coming right now' (zhe jiu lai).

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 把这些高频的短句练熟，形成肌肉记忆。等肌肉记忆多了，语感自然就来了。
segments.speech_text: 把这些高频的短句练熟，形成肌肉记忆。等肌肉记忆多了，语感自然就来了。
segments.en: Practice these high-frequency short phrases until they're smooth, building muscle memory. Once you have enough muscle memory, your language sense will naturally follow.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 盼盼总结得太棒了。先模仿，后理解，这才是学习语言最有效的方法。
segments.speech_text: [warmly] 盼盼总结得太棒了。先模仿，后理解，这才是学习语言最有效的方法。
segments.en: Panpan summarized that brilliantly. Mimic first, understand later—that is truly the most effective way to learn a language.

blocks.purpose: 总结本集并鼓励听众互动订阅

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 没错，语言是用出来的，不是算出来的。希望大家以后不要再害怕这三个小词了。
segments.speech_text: [confidently] 没错，语言是用出来的，不是算出来的。希望大家以后不要再害怕这三个小词了。
segments.en: Exactly, language is something you use, not something you calculate. I hope everyone will stop being afraid of these three little words from now on.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 如果大家觉得今天的节目对你有帮助，请一定记得给我们的播客点赞、订阅，并分享给同样在学习中文的朋友。
segments.speech_text: 如果大家觉得今天的节目对你有帮助，请一定记得给我们的播客点赞、订阅，并分享给同样在学习中文的朋友。
segments.en: If you all felt today's episode was helpful, please make sure to like and subscribe to our podcast, and share it with friends who are also learning Chinese.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 大家在日常生活中，有听到什么有趣的带有“了、把、就”的句子吗？欢迎在评论区留言和我们分享。
segments.speech_text: 大家在日常生活中，有听到什么有趣的带有“了、把、就”的句子吗？欢迎在评论区留言和我们分享。
segments.en: Have you guys heard any interesting sentences containing 'le, ba, or jiu' in your daily lives? Welcome to leave a comment and share with us in the comment section.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 感谢大家的收听与陪伴。
segments.speech_text: [happy] 感谢大家的收听与陪伴。
segments.en: Thank you all for listening and keeping us company.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 我们下期节目再见啦！拜拜！
segments.speech_text: 我们下期节目再见啦！拜拜！
segments.en: We'll see you in the next episode! Bye-bye!

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 再见。
segments.speech_text: 再见。
segments.en: Goodbye.
